seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning on the south-facing slopes, January to February
seasonal_wine.description: Through the coldest weeks, growers prune the bare south-facing slopes between Hochheim and Lorch by hand, shaping next year's canopy. Cellars are quiet, with the young vintage settling on its lees and the steep terraces stripped back to vine and stone.
seasonal_wine.tip: Winter is the easiest time to book a slow, unhurried estate tasting; many Rheingau growers are at home and happy to pour older library vintages.

seasonal_wine.name: Eiswein harvest window, December to January
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual but vintage-dependent, December to January
seasonal_wine.description: When the temperature drops below minus seven, the few Rheingau growers who left grapes hanging rush to pick frozen bunches before dawn for Eiswein, pressing concentrated juice while the water stays as ice. Warm winters now mean some years deliver no Eiswein at all.
seasonal_wine.tip: Eiswein is a weather gamble; ask Rheingau estates whether the current vintage actually produced any, as several recent warm winters delivered none.

seasonal_wine.name: Christmas markets and Gluehwein season, December
seasonal_wine.description: From late November Rheingau towns from Eltville to Ruedesheim and Wiesbaden open Christmas markets, with stalls pouring Gluehwein made from local Riesling and Spaetburgunder spiced with cinnamon, cloves and citrus zest under the half-timbered facades.
seasonal_wine.tip: Ruedesheim's Drosselgasse and Wiesbaden's Schlossplatz markets are the busiest; Eltville and Hochheim run smaller, slower Christmas markets if you want quieter Gluehwein nights.

seasonal_wine.name: Bud break on the south slopes, April
seasonal_wine.description: As April warms the south-facing slopes, the first green shoots break along the Rheingau terraces and the steep walls of Schloss Johannisberg, the Steinberg and the Hattenheim hills turn from grey to fresh leaf, with growers tying down canes for the long season ahead.
seasonal_wine.tip: Spring light on the budding south-facing slopes makes for the best vineyard photography of the year before the canopy fills in by early summer.

seasonal_wine.name: Rheingau Musik Festival, late June to early September
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, late June to early September
seasonal_wine.description: From late June through early September the Rheingau Musik Festival fills Kloster Eberbach, Schloss Johannisberg, Schloss Vollrads and other estates with classical, jazz and crossover concerts, many pairing the music with estate Riesling and Spaetburgunder.
seasonal_wine.tip: Buy seats early for concerts inside the Kloster Eberbach basilica; the acoustics inside the 12th-century church are the headline of the season.

seasonal_wine.name: Rheingauer Weinwoche in Wiesbaden, August
seasonal_wine.description: For ten days every August the Schlossplatz and Dern'sches Gelaende in Wiesbaden host the Rheingauer Weinwoche, with around 100 stands pouring Rheingau Riesling, Spaetburgunder and Sekt directly from growers across the region.
seasonal_wine.tip: Pace yourself across two evenings; the 100-stand layout is too big for a single visit, and the Schlossplatz cools fast after dark.

seasonal_wine.name: Riesling harvest in the Rheingau, late September to November
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, late September to November with selective passes
seasonal_wine.description: The cornerstone Rheingau season. From late September pickers make multiple passes up the south-facing slopes to sort grapes for everything from bone-dry GG to nobly sweet Auslese, choosing each bunch by ripeness and botrytis as Riesling hangs into November.
seasonal_wine.tip: Harvest visits must be arranged directly with a grower in advance; the cellars are flat-out, but watching selective picking above Kloster Eberbach is the trip's best memory.

seasonal_wine.name: Federweisser and Zwiebelkuchen, autumn
seasonal_wine.description: As the first must ferments, cloudy fizzy Federweisser appears in Rheingau taverns and at the autumn village fests, traditionally drunk with warm Zwiebelkuchen onion tart. It is the taste of the Rheingau autumn and the heart of harvest-season events from Hochheim to Ruedesheim.
seasonal_wine.tip: Federweisser keeps fermenting in the bottle, so never seal it tight; drink it within a day or two and always pair with warm onion tart at any autumn village fest.

seasonal_wine.name: VDP Grosses Gewaechs release, early September
seasonal_wine.description: Every September the VDP releases its dry Grosses Gewaechs Rieslings from the previous vintage at a national pre-release tasting, a fixed point on the German calendar that drops Rheingau GGs from Robert Weil, Kuenstler, Leitz, Spreitzer and others onto the market.
seasonal_wine.tip: Rheingau GGs are made in small lots; if you want a specific Hattenheim or Hochheim site, order from the estate or a specialist as soon as the September release lands.

seasonal_wine.name: Kloster Eberbach autumn tastings, late autumn
seasonal_wine.description: From late October the Hessische Staatsweingueter at Kloster Eberbach runs an autumn programme of vintage tastings, vertical Spaetburgunder dinners and Steinberg single-site flights in the cellars and refectory of the 12th-century abbey.
seasonal_wine.tip: Tasting nights in the medieval Hospital cellars or the Lay refectory book up fast; check the Kloster Eberbach events calendar a season in advance.
